title: 401(k) Plans - Features and Rules
description: Explain 401(k) contribution limits and catch-up provisions
topics: Explain 401(k) contribution limits and catch-up provisions
Apply vesting schedules and employer matching

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: The 401(k) is the most common employer-sponsored retirement plan, named after the Internal Revenue Code section that created it. It's available for for-profit companies and allows employee deferrals plus employer contributions.

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: 401(k) plans are the primary retirement savings vehicle for most Americans. Understanding contribution limits, matching, and distribution rules is essential for maximizing retirement savings.

sections.title: SECURE 2.0 Enhanced Catch-Up
sections.content: Starting 2025, ages 60-63 get higher catch-up: $10,000 or 150% of regular catch-up (approximately $11,250). This creates additional savings opportunity for those close to retirement.

sections.title: Traditional vs. Roth 401(k)
rows: Lower bracket now or same/higher later

sections.title: Roth 401(k) Qualified Distribution
sections.content: Tax-free if: (1) Account held 5 years from first contribution, AND (2) Age 59½, death, or disability.

sections.title: Employer Contributions
items: Common match formulas: 50% on first 6% = 3%, or 100% on first 3% + 50% on next 2% = 4%
Safe Harbor 401(k): 3% non-elective OR 100% on first 3% + 50% on next 2% (4% total)
Safe harbor contributions must be immediately vested
Profit Sharing: Discretionary, up to 25% of eligible payroll

sections.title: Exam Tip
sections.content: Employee contributions are ALWAYS 100% vested immediately. Vesting schedules only apply to employer contributions.

sections.title: Distribution Triggering Events
items: Separation from service (any age)
Age 59½ (while still employed)
Disability
Death
Plan termination
Hardship (special rules)

sections.title: Exceptions to 10% Early Withdrawal Penalty
rows: Separation from service in year turning 55+
Unable to engage in gainful activity

sections.title: 401(k) Loans
sections.content: Limit: Lesser of 50% of vested balance OR $50,000 (reduced by highest loan balance in prior 12 months). Must repay within 5 years (longer for home purchase) with level amortization. Default = deemed distribution (taxable + penalty if under 59½).

sections.title: Required Minimum Distributions
sections.content: RMD Starting Age: 73 (SECURE 2.0 for those turning 72 after 2022), Age 75 starting in 2033. Still-Working Exception: No RMDs if still employed by sponsoring employer AND not a 5%+ owner. RMD = Account Balance (Dec 31 prior year) / Life Expectancy Factor.

sections.title: SECURE 2.0 Updates
rows: Employer can match student loan payments

sections.title: Summary
items: 2026 limits: $24,500 employee ($32,000 with catch-up); $71,500 total
Roth 401(k): After-tax in, tax-free out (if qualified)
Age 55 rule: Penalty-free withdrawals at separation after 55
Safe harbor: 3% non-elective or 4% match, immediately vested
RMDs start: Age 73 (75 in 2033)

knowledgeCheck.question: In 2025, assuming the regular catch-up contribution limit for those age 50 and over remains at $7,500, what is the maximum catch-up contribution amount for an individual aged 62, as allowed by SECURE 2.0's enhanced catch-up provision?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The SECURE 2.0 Act stipulates that those aged 60-63 can contribute the greater of $10,000 or 150% of the regular catch-up amount. In this case, 150% of $7,500 is $11,250, which is greater than $10,000. Therefore, the maximum catch-up contribution is $11,250.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to calculate 150% of the regular catch-up amount and compare it to the $10,000 threshold.

rows.label: Contribution Tax Treatment
values: Pre-tax contributions; reduces taxable income in the year of contribution.
After-tax contributions; no immediate tax benefit.

rows.label: Withdrawal Tax Treatment (in retirement)
values: Taxable withdrawals in retirement.
Qualified withdrawals are tax-free in retirement (principal and earnings).

rows.label: Impact on Current vs. Future Tax Liability
values: Lowers current taxable income, potentially higher taxes in retirement.
Higher current taxable income, potentially no taxes on qualified withdrawals in retirement.

reveal.question: Define 'vesting' in the context of a 401(k) plan, and explain its significance for an employee.
reveal.answer: Vesting refers to an employee's right to employer-matching contributions or profit sharing in a retirement plan. It determines when the employee has full ownership of these contributions. Until fully vested, the employee might forfeit a portion of the employer contributions if they leave the company. Vesting schedules dictate how quickly an employee gains ownership.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: 403(b) and 457 plans serve specific employer types with unique rules that differ from traditional 401(k)s. Understanding these differences is crucial for planning.

sections.title: 403(b) Plans - Tax-Sheltered Annuities
sections.content: Eligible employers: Public schools (K-12 and higher education), Tax-exempt 501(c)(3) organizations (hospitals, charities), Churches and religious organizations. Same contribution limits as 401(k): Under 50: $24,500, Age 50+: $32,000, Total annual addition: $71,500.

sections.title: 403(b) 15-Year Catch-Up
sections.content: Employees with 15+ years of service with same employer get additional $3,000/year (lifetime max $15,000). Must be used BEFORE age 50 catch-up. Maximum possible: $24,500 + $3,000 + $7,500 = $35,000.

sections.title: 403(b) Investment Options
items: Annuities (from insurance companies)
Mutual funds (custodial accounts)
Church retirement accounts (for churches only)
Individual stocks NOT permitted in 403(b) plans

sections.title: Key 457(b) Advantage
sections.content: Governmental 457(b) has NO 10% early withdrawal penalty regardless of age at separation. This makes it excellent for early retirees!

sections.title: 457(b) Special 3-Year Catch-Up
sections.content: In the 3 years before normal retirement age, can contribute up to double the limit (2026: up to $49,000). Cannot use both 3-year catch-up AND age 50 catch-up in same year.

sections.title: Summary
items: 403(b): For non-profits/schools; 15-year catch-up available
457(b) governmental: NO 10% early withdrawal penalty
457(b) non-governmental: Not rollover eligible to IRA; not creditor protected
Dual contribution: 401(k)/403(b) + 457(b) = separate limits ($49,000 total if under 50)
3-year catch-up: 457(b) allows double limit in final 3 years

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following employers is ELIGIBLE to sponsor a 403(b) plan?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Public schools (K-12 and higher education) are eligible to sponsor 403(b) plans. For-profit entities and most privately held companies generally sponsor 401(k) plans.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about the types of organizations that are typically tax-exempt.

reveal.question: What is the 403(b) contribution limit for an individual under age 50 in 2023?
reveal.answer: $22,500 (Same as 401k). The 2024 limit is $23,000. Knowing the yearly contribution limits is crucial for the CFP exam.

rows.label: Eligible Employers
values: Public schools, 501(c)(3) organizations, Churches
Any for-profit or non-profit organization

rows.label: Investment Options
values: Annuities and mutual funds
Wider range of investments including stocks, bonds, and mutual funds

title: Defined Benefit Pension Plans
description: Explain how defined benefit pensions work
topics: Explain how defined benefit pensions work
Calculate pension benefits using common formulas
Evaluate lump sum vs. annuity pension options

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Defined Benefit (DB) plans provide a guaranteed lifetime income based on a formula, rather than account balance. The employer bears both investment and longevity risk.

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: While fewer employees have DB plans today, understanding them remains important. Many clients have old pensions, and DB plans are making a comeback for high-income business owners.

sections.title: Common Benefit Formulas
sections.content: Flat Dollar: Annual Benefit = Years of Service × Dollar Amount (e.g., $75 × 30 years = $2,250/month). Career Average: Benefit = Years × Multiplier × Career Average Salary. Final Average (most common): Benefit = Years × Multiplier × Final Average (highest 3-5 years). Example: 30 years × 1.5% × $100,000 = $45,000/year.

sections.title: Exam Tip
sections.content: ERISA requires QJSA (Qualified Joint and Survivor Annuity) as default for married participants. At least 50% continues to surviving spouse. Spouse must consent to waive.

sections.title: PBGC Protection
sections.content: PBGC insures private-sector defined benefit plans. 2026 Maximum Insured Benefit at age 65: $7,108/month ($85,295/year). Benefits exceeding PBGC limits, plans less than 5 years old, and health/welfare benefits are NOT covered.

sections.title: Cash Balance Plans (Hybrid)
sections.content: Looks like a DC plan (individual account) but IS a DB plan (employer bears risk). Account grows with Pay Credits (% of salary) and Interest Credits (guaranteed rate). Example: 5% pay credit on $100,000 = $5,000/year + 4% interest on balance.

sections.title: Summary
items: DB formula: Years × Multiplier × Final Average Salary (commonly)
Employer bears all investment and longevity risk
Lump sum suits short life expectancy, legacy goals, investment confidence
Annuity suits long life expectancy, guaranteed income needs, risk aversion
PBGC insures up to ~$85,300/year (2026) at age 65

sections.title: Defined Benefit Pension Plan Formula Practice

knowledgeCheck.question: Sarah is retiring after 25 years of service. Her defined benefit pension plan uses a final average salary formula, calculating benefits as 1.75% of her final average salary for each year of service. Her final average salary, calculated from her highest three years, is $120,000. What is Sarah's expected annual pension benefit?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The correct answer is $60,000. The calculation is: 25 years * 1.75% * $120,000 = $52,500. Therefore, the expected annual pension benefit is $52,500. However, the question asks for the *monthly* benefit. Divide the annual benefit by 12: $52,500 / 12 = $4,375
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to multiply years of service by the percentage multiplier by the final average salary to find the annual benefit.

reveal.question: In a defined benefit pension plan, who bears the investment risk and the longevity risk?
reveal.answer: The employer bears both the investment risk (the risk that investments underperform) and the longevity risk (the risk that retirees live longer than expected).

sections.title: Lump Sum vs. Annuity: Key Considerations

rows.label: Investment Control
values: Participant has full control over investment decisions.
Employer/Pension Plan controls investment decisions.

rows.label: Longevity Risk
values: Participant assumes longevity risk; may outlive savings.
Pension plan assumes longevity risk; guaranteed lifetime income.

rows.label: Inflation Risk
values: Can be mitigated through investment strategies (e.g., inflation-protected securities).
May or may not have inflation adjustments (COLA); fixed income may erode over time.

rows.label: PBGC Protection
values: Not applicable after distribution.
Protected up to PBGC limits, but coverage is not guaranteed in all situations.

title: Profit Sharing and Stock Bonus Plans
description: Explain profit sharing plan features and contribution rules
topics: Explain profit sharing plan features and contribution rules
Distinguish ESOPs from other stock bonus plans

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Profit sharing and stock bonus plans are employer-funded retirement vehicles that provide flexible contribution options without requiring employee deferrals.

sections.title: Profit Sharing Plans
rows: Amount can vary year to year

sections.title: Allocation Methods
items: Pro-Rata (most common): Same percentage of compensation to all participants
New Comparability (age-weighted): Allocates more to older, higher-paid employees
Integrated with Social Security: Higher contribution % above SS wage base

sections.title: Pro-Rata Example
sections.content: $100,000 profit sharing pool with $500,000 total eligible compensation. Allocation rate = 20%. Employee earning $75,000 receives: $75,000 × 20% = $15,000.

sections.title: Stock Bonus Plans
sections.content: Similar to profit sharing but contributions made in company stock. Benefits distributed in company stock. Participant bears stock price risk. Creates employee ownership culture.

sections.title: Employee Stock Ownership Plans (ESOPs)
rows: Can borrow to buy company stock
Must invest primarily in employer stock
Employees can sell stock back to company

sections.title: Section 1042 Rollover
sections.content: C-corporation selling shareholders can sell 30%+ of company to ESOP, reinvest in qualified replacement property, and defer capital gains indefinitely. Requirements: Stock held 3+ years, C-corporation, seller cannot participate as employee afterwards.

sections.title: ESOP Diversification Rights
sections.content: After age 55 and 10 years of participation: Can diversify up to 25% of account (years 1-5), up to 50% in final year. This protects employees from concentration risk.

sections.title: Summary
items: Profit sharing: 100% employer-funded, discretionary, up to 25% of payroll
Allocation methods: Pro-rata, age-weighted/new comparability, SS-integrated
ESOPs: Can leverage (borrow), must invest in employer stock
Section 1042: C-corp sellers can defer capital gains into replacement property
ESOP diversification: 25% after age 55 + 10 years; 50% in final year

knowledgeCheck.question: An employer sponsors a profit sharing plan and allocates contributions using a pro-rata method. The company's total profit sharing pool is $250,000, and the total eligible compensation for all employees is $1,000,000. Sarah earns $80,000. What is Sarah's profit sharing allocation?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The allocation rate is calculated as $250,000 / $1,000,000 = 25%. Sarah's allocation is $80,000 * 25% = $20,000.
knowledgeCheck.hint: First, calculate the allocation rate based on the total profit sharing pool and total eligible compensation.

reveal.question: Name at least two tax benefits specific to ESOPs (Employee Stock Ownership Plans) that are NOT typically found in other stock bonus or profit sharing plans.
reveal.answer: 1. **Tax-deductible dividends:** Dividends paid on ESOP stock are tax-deductible to the company if they are used to repay the ESOP loan or passed through to participants. 2. **Deferral of capital gains:** Under certain conditions, a seller of company stock to an ESOP can defer capital gains if the proceeds are reinvested in qualified replacement property. 3. **S-Corp ESOPs:** An ESOP can be the sole shareholder of an S-Corporation, potentially shielding profits from income tax at the corporate level (although distributions to employee-owners are still taxed).

sections.title: Profit Sharing vs. Stock Bonus Plans

rows.label: Contribution Type
values: Cash or company stock
Primarily company stock

rows.label: Distribution Type
values: Cash, company stock, or other property
Primarily company stock

rows.label: Investment Risk
values: Participant can diversify investments (if allowed)
Participant bears the risk of company stock performance
